name: cassandra-driver (DataStax Apache Cassandra Python Driver)

performance.notes: Cassandra is optimized for write-heavy workloads; P99 depends heavily on cluster size and consistency level

agent_readiness.notes: Official Apache Cassandra Python driver. from cassandra.cluster import Cluster; from cassandra.auth import PlainTextAuthProvider. auth = PlainTextAuthProvider('user', 'pass'). cluster = Cluster(['127.0.0.1'], auth_provider=auth). session = cluster.connect('my_keyspace'). rows = session.execute('SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = %s', (uuid,)). Prepared: stmt = session.prepare('INSERT INTO t (id, name) VALUES (?, ?)'); session.execute(stmt, (id, name)). Async: session.execute_async(query).add_callback(fn). ORM: from cassandra.cqlengine import columns, models. Batch: BatchStatement. Lightweight transactions. Object mapper (cqlengine) for Cassandra models. C extension for performance.

gotchas: Cassandra's distributed model requires understanding consistency levels (ONE, QUORUM, ALL)
Schema design is query-driven — model tables around your queries
cqlengine ORM is less flexible than SQLAlchemy — Cassandra-specific patterns
No JOIN support — denormalization is required
C extension (libev or libuv) needed for best async performance
avoid_when: Small-scale applications where PostgreSQL is sufficient
Ad-hoc queries without pre-defined access patterns
summary: cassandra-driver is the official DataStax Python driver for Apache Cassandra. from cassandra.cluster import Cluster; cluster = Cluster(['host']); session = cluster.connect('keyspace'); rows = session.execute('SELECT * FROM t WHERE id = %s', (id,)). Prepared statements. Async. ORM via cqlengine. Apache-2.0 by DataStax. Best for distributed write-heavy workloads and IoT time-series. Schema must be query-driven — no JOINs.
